Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after work or on weekends, but it rarely happened in any organized way. Most of my studying was reactive—I'd realize something was due and scramble to get it done. I'd maybe spend a few hours here and there, but it was scattered and unfocused. I know I should have been more disciplined, but I just couldn't seem to make it stick.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then when a deadline was coming up I'd suddenly be online trying to catch up. I work part-time and my schedule changes, so finding consistent study times was hard. I'd often end up studying late at night when I was already tired, which wasn't very effective.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder email or seeing a notification that something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important. The gamified task thing they introduced actually got me logging in more often because there were points and badges involved, which made it feel less boring. But mostly it was panic about assignments that got me there.
Q4: For a little while, the gamified elements made me want to check in more regularly to see my progress and earn badges. It became almost like checking social media—just a quick look to see what was new. But that wore off after a few weeks when I realized the badges didn't really help my actual grades. The habit didn't stick because my motivation was never really there to begin with.
Q5: The reading materials and text-based resources were most useful to me because I learn better by reading than watching videos. I liked being able to highlight things and take notes in my own words. The discussion boards were okay when people actually posted helpful stuff, but that was hit or miss. Honestly, most things felt like they were just going through the motions.
Q6: I barely watched the videos—they took too long and I'd zone out halfway through. I'd skip the optional readings entirely because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. Some of the extra practice exercises I ignored because I figured if they weren't graded, why bother when I was already overwhelmed with everything else.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly tied to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. The gamified tasks were kind of fun at first, but once I realized they didn't actually count for much, my interest dropped. I needed to see a clear payoff to feel like something was worth the effort.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them—like I had to do them or face consequences. Optional things felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on when I was already behind on the mandatory stuff. It's not that I didn't see their value, I just didn't have the energy or motivation to go beyond what was absolutely necessary.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download the PDFs and readings to my phone so I could read them on my bus ride to work. I took handwritten notes sometimes because typing felt too impersonal. I also used some external websites to help explain concepts that the course materials didn't make clear enough. None of that would show up in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd read through my printed notes for an hour or more, trying to make sense of things, but obviously the LMS wouldn't know about that. I'd also think about course concepts while doing other things—like trying to connect ideas while I was at work. Sometimes learning happens in your head, not on a screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF, open it once in the LMS so it registered, then read it multiple times offline over the next few days. The LMS would only show that one initial access, but I might have spent hours with that same document. It was just easier to work with files on my own device.
Q12: Around midterms everything got harder. I was working more hours because I needed the money, and I was falling behind in multiple classes. The stress built up but I didn't feel anxious exactly—more like numb and disconnected. I'd look at the course materials and just feel nothing, like I couldn't make myself care even though I knew I should.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities made it impossible to give the course the attention it needed. Sometimes I'd be so mentally drained that even simple tasks felt overwhelming, so I'd just close the laptop and tell myself I'd do it tomorrow.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the course page open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I was technically 'active' in the system but I wasn't actually learning anything. It was like going through the motions without any real engagement.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments constantly because they felt too overwhelming to start. I knew they were important, but the thought of tackling them made me want to avoid them even more. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd find another excuse. It was this cycle I couldn't break out of.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd wait until the last possible moment, then rush to complete whatever was due. The LMS would probably show these spikes of activity right before deadlines and then nothing for days. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I functioned.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally to the next crisis. Sometimes the feedback was discouraging because it confirmed I wasn't doing well, which just made me want to disengage more. I know feedback is supposed to help, but it mostly just reminded me how behind I was.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which didn't work great for my chaotic life. Weekly modules with specific deadlines meant I was always playing catch-up. If the structure had been more flexible, I might have been able to work at my own pace during my less busy weeks. Instead, I was constantly behind.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier at first—they gave me small goals to work toward. But the overall pacing was too fast for me, and there wasn't much flexibility for people with jobs and other responsibilities. The lack of low-stakes practice opportunities made everything feel high-pressure, which made me want to avoid it.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in a lot, clicks around, maybe earns some badges or checks discussions, but then can't bring themselves to actually complete the assignments. The activity is there but the follow-through isn't. It might look like engagement but it's really just... presence without purpose.
Q21: I can see how that would happen if someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material. For me, I had decent LMS activity but my performance was poor, so I'm kind of the opposite. But I get that the numbers don't always match reality—someone could be learning effectively without the system knowing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you log in, what you click on, how long pages are open. But they can't show if you're actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss the offline studying, the mental processing, the real learning that happens away from the screen. They're just tracking behavior, not comprehension.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and invested in your own education. For me, I was never really engaged this semester. I was just surviving, trying to get through without completely failing, which I did anyway.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how disconnected and unmotivated I felt all semester. It wouldn't show that I was working to pay rent and had no energy left for school. It wouldn't capture the guilt I felt every time I avoided an assignment, or how much I actually wanted to do better but couldn't seem to make it happen. The data shows what I did, not why I couldn't do more.
